input.prompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the
JSON output.

I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework

data.reasoningText:
The user wants a .NET 11 console app that serializes a `Person` record to JSON with PascalCase property names using built-in framework features.

In .NET 9+, `System.Text.Json` added `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` as a built-in naming policy. Since the record has lowercase parameter names (`name`, `age`), C# will generate properties with those same names (lowercase). To get PascalCase output, we can use `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase`.

Wait, let me think about this more carefully. In C#, positional record parameters like `public record Person(string name, int age)` generate properties with the same casing as the parameter names. 

So the properties would be `name` and `age` (lowercase). To serialize them as `Name` and `Age`, I need a PascalCase naming policy. I'm trying to recall when `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` was actually added to the framework—I know .NET 8 introduced several other naming policies like SnakeCaseLower and KebabCaseLower, but I'm not entirely certain about PascalCase specifically.

Let me think through what's actually available. `CamelCase` has been there from the start, and without any naming policy, System.Text.Json just preserves the original property names as-is. Since I'm uncertain about what's available in .NET 11, I should probably just try creating the project and testing it directly to see what APIs are actually there. The record parameters generate properties matching the parameter names exactly, so `Person(string name, int age)` creates lowercase properties. To get PascalCase JSON output, I could either define the record with PascalCase parameters like `Person(string Name, int Age)`, or use a naming policy converter. Let me try the built-in `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` and see if it works.

data.content: Good — .NET 11 preview 3 is available. Let me create the project and test.
